1: The assembly at Mizpah (hammiṣpāh H4709, 'watchtower/lookout') — the site is identified with Tel en-Naṣbeh, approximately 12 km north of Jerusalem in the territory of Benjamin. Mizpah was a recurring assembly point in pre-monarchic Israel: Samuel will later assemble Israel here (1 Samuel 7:5-12; Judges 10:17) and it will serve as Saul's mustering point. The assembly formula kəʾîš ʾeḥāḏ ('as one man') appears four times in Judges (Judges 6:16; Judges 20:1,8,11) and is the standard idiom for unanimous tribal solidarity. Dan to Beersheba is the standard formula for the full north-south extent of Israel's settled land (ca. 240 km), with Gilead representing the Transjordanian tribes. The assembly of the entire nation for a judicial proceeding parallels ANE practices of national assemblies (puhrum in Akkadian sources) summoned to adjudicate matters of national significance.
2: 400,000 swordsmen — the census figure for the muster. ANE military censuses are common in royal inscriptions (Shalmaneser III records coalition forces at Qarqar in 853 BCE), and large coalition forces were feasible for major campaigns. Whether this figure is literal or conventional, the narrative's point is a massive response: nearly all of Israel's military capacity is mobilized against one tribe. The leaders of all the people (pinnôt kol-hāʿām) taking their stand reflects the formal structure of the tribal assembly: tribal chiefs and clan leaders as formal representatives, not merely a random gathering.
3: The Benjaminites heard of Israel's assembly — the reporting of the muster to Benjamin is not an ultimatum but intelligence reaching them through normal communication channels. Benjamin's geographic position is central: their territory lay between Ephraim and Judah, bordered the Jordan Valley to the east, and encompassed Jerusalem (though the city was not yet fully Israelite). Gibeah of Benjamin (identified with Tell el-Ful, 5 km north of Jerusalem) was the site of the crime. The Benjaminite question — 'How did this evil happen?' — is a formal legal inquiry (ʾêk niqtāh hārāʿāh hazzōʾt), the opening of a judicial proceeding.
4: The Levite's account to the Mizpah assembly — he identifies himself as 'the husband of the murdered woman' and as a resident of the farthest part of the hill country of Ephraim. The phrase 'came to Gibeah of Benjamin' (giḇʿatāh binyāmîn) locates the crime in Benjamin's territory, which has legal significance: Benjamin bears communal responsibility for crimes occurring within their tribal allocation (Deuteronomy 21:1-9 establishes the principle of communal responsibility for an unresolved murder). The Levite's testimony is the formal accusation in the tribal legal proceeding.
5: The Levite's account of the night attack — the lords of Gibeah (baʿălê haggibʿāh) surrounded the house and intended to kill him. He omits significant details from Judges 19 (the host's offer of his daughter; his own role in thrusting the concubine outside) — the account is self-serving. The phrase lizkôt lî ('intended to kill me') frames the crime as attempted murder of the Levite rather than the rape-murder of his concubine. ANE legal testimony in assembly proceedings was typically a plaintiff's narrative, not a sworn deposition — the listener was expected to ask follow-up questions.
6: The Levite's action of cutting his concubine into twelve pieces and sending them throughout Israel's territory — this is the only narrative action described in his testimony that matches Judges 19. The gesture has ANE parallels: at Mari (18th century BCE), a dismembered donkey was sent through the territory to summon warriors to a muster (the 'donkey-killing' ritual for treaty obligations). The twelve pieces sent to all twelve tribal territories is a claim on the covenant solidarity of all Israel. The gesture bypasses normal judicial channels and makes an emotional-political appeal directly to the entire people.
7: The Levite's closing appeal: 'Here you are, all people of Israel; give your advice and counsel here' (hinnēkem kūllākem bənê yiśrāʾēl hābû lākem dābār wāʿēṣāh pōh). This is a formal call for communal deliberation — the assembly format requires a collective decision. The appeal invokes the covenant solidarity of 'all the people of Israel' as the relevant deliberating body. In ANE tribal assemblies, judicial decisions required communal consensus; Gideon's encounter with the Israelite tribes follows a similar pattern of appealing to national solidarity for a collective military-judicial response.
8: The unanimous response: 'Not one of us shall go to his tent; not one of us shall return to his house' — the assembly invokes the ancient sacral war formula of commitment: no departure until the matter is resolved. kəʾîš ʾeḥāḏ ('as one man') — the solidarity formula repeated. The decision to wage war on Gibeah is framed as a sacred obligation analogous to holy war: the assembly has heard the testimony, identified the guilty party, and unanimously decided that Gibeah's crime requires national-level response. The tone is judicial-military: this is execution of communal justice, not personal revenge.
9: The assembly's military planning: casting lots (nāʿal bigôrāl H1486) to determine which units provision the army. The lot (gôrāl) was used throughout ancient Israel for decision-making (land allocation in Joshua; selection of Saul as king in 1 Sam 10; Jonah cast lots). The lot-casting here divides the assembly into two groups: fighters and provisioners. The logistics of a 400,000-man army required approximately 1/10th in supply roles — the one-in-ten ratio (10% provisioning, 90% combat) is historically plausible for a pre-industrial army on a short campaign.
10: The supply calculation: 10 men per 100, 100 per 1,000, 1,000 per 10,000 — a proportional levy system. The army provision plan targets Gibeah of Benjamin specifically — the campaign objective is not Benjamin as a whole but the specific city responsible for the crime. The provisioning structure suggests a professional military organization: detachments, proportional levy, and a named campaign objective. The purpose clause — 'to repay Gibeah of Benjamin for all the outrage that they have committed in Israel' — is a judicial declaration of the casus belli.
11: The mustering formula: 'So all the men of Israel gathered against the city, united as one man' (ʾîš ʾeḥāḏ ḥăḇērîm). ḥāḇēr (H2270, joined/allied — the word implies a formal alliance, as in a confederacy) — the standard amphictyonic solidarity is invoked. Whether Israel had a formal amphictyony (twelve-tribe league centered on a central sanctuary, as proposed by Noth and others) is debated; but the narrative assumes a covenant-solidarity that binds all tribes to mutual defense and mutual justice. The assembly at Mizpah and the unanimous commitment exemplify this principle in its starkest form.
12: Israel sends messengers throughout all Benjamin demanding the surrender of the men of Gibeah. This is a formal ultimatum following the standard ANE pattern of demand-before-attack: military action was considered illegitimate unless preceded by a formal demand for redress. The messengers address all the tribe rather than just Gibeah, because the crime falls within Benjamin's tribal jurisdiction and Benjamin bears corporate responsibility for resolving it (Deuteronomy 21:2: the elders of the nearest city to an unresolved murder bear responsibility). Benjamin's refusal to surrender the men triggers the war.
13: The demand: deliver the worthless fellows (bənê-bəliyyaʿal H1100, sons of worthlessness/Belial) — the legal formula for persons who are morally outside the covenant community and subject to communal death (Deuteronomy 13:13-15: the formula for dealing with a city that has apostatized). Israel invokes the Deuteronomic purge formula: ûbāʿartā rāʿ miyyiśrāʾēl ('and you shall purge the evil from Israel') appears six times in Deuteronomy for capital crimes. Benjamin's refusal to listen to their brothers makes the tribe collectively guilty: they shield the perpetrators, making themselves accessories after the fact under ANE legal norms.
14: Benjamin musters from their cities to Gibeah — the tribe mobilizes for war rather than surrender. The Benjaminite muster is itself a signal that the tribe has chosen corporate solidarity with Gibeah over covenant justice with the rest of Israel. 26,000 swordsmen plus 700 from Gibeah (vv15-16) — a tribal force of approximately 26,700 men opposing an Israelite force of 400,000. The military asymmetry is enormous, but the Benjaminites were known warriors (Genesis 49:27: 'Benjamin is a ravenous wolf'). Saul (a Benjaminite) and his warriors will later demonstrate the tribe's martial capability.
15: 26,000 swordsmen plus the inhabitants of Gibeah — the dual count distinguishes between the general tribal levy and the specific Gibeah contingent that includes the men being sought. The Benjaminite muster is from 'their cities' (mēʿārîm) — suggesting a systematic mobilization of the entire tribe rather than just one city's forces. The tribal military capacity of Benjamin in this period is consistent with Genesis 49:27's characterization: a small but fierce tribe. That they muster despite being grossly outnumbered suggests either extreme tribal loyalty, calculation that YHWH would favor them, or underestimation of Israel's resolve.
16: The 700 left-handed slingers from Gibeah (šebaʿ-mēʾôt ʾîš bāḥûr ʾiṭṭēr yad-yəmînô) — literally 'bound/restricted of right hand' — is a remarkable detail. The Benjaminites were known for left-handed warriors (Ehud in 3:15 is also a Benjaminite left-hander). The sling was one of the most effective ranged weapons of antiquity: Assyrian reliefs show professional slingers in battlefield formations, and range of 200+ meters with accuracy was achievable. 700 accurate slingers at a critical moment could inflict devastating casualties. The phrase 'every one could sling a stone at a hair and not miss' is a standard formula for elite proficiency.
17: Israel's total: 400,000 swordsmen — the narrative restates the count to create contrast with Benjamin's 26,700. The ratio is approximately 15:1 in Israel's favor. Yet the first two battles result in devastating Israelite losses (22,000 in v21; 18,000 in v25), demonstrating that numerical superiority does not guarantee victory without YHWH's active participation. The narrative's theological point is that YHWH's delayed victory (granting Benjamin two improbable victories before the third battle) serves his larger purpose of both chastising Israel and ultimately fulfilling justice.
18: Israel's first consultation at Bethel (bêt-ʾēl) — the location of the ark of the covenant in this period (v27-28 confirms Phinehas ministered there). Bethel (identified with Beitin, 17 km north of Jerusalem) had been a major sanctuary since Jacob's vision (Genesis 28:19). The consultation format — asking which tribe should go up first — parallels Judges 1:1's opening consultation about conquering Canaan. YHWH's answer designates Judah, as in Judges 1:2. The tribal lottery for who leads is the standard pre-battle consultation that maintains YHWH's sovereign direction of the campaign.
19: Israel sets out from their camp (wayyāqām bənê yiśrāʾēl babbōqer) — the dawn departure is the standard military timing for ancient battles: armies moved at first light to maximize daylight combat hours. Against Gibeah — the campaign target remains the specific city rather than Benjamin as a tribe. The deployment against a walled city in the hill country required specific tactics: the Benjaminite slingers (v16) would have been devastating to troops attempting to assault Gibeah's walls or approach positions.
20: Israel draws up for battle against Benjamin at Gibeah. The battle formation (wayyaʿarəkû milḥāmāh — the same verb as setting a table; 'arranging' a battle) implies a formal tactical deployment, not a charge. The approach to a fortified Benjaminite position would require infantry formations that could resist the elite Benjaminite slingers while maintaining cohesion. The terrain around Tell el-Ful (proposed Gibeah site) includes valleys and ridges that would have channeled approach routes and given defenders significant advantage.
21: 22,000 Israelites fell on the first day — a catastrophic defeat for the numerically superior force. The loss of 22,000 from a 400,000-man army represents approximately 5.5% battlefield casualties, which would be devastating by ancient standards. First-day defeats against defending forces were common: the defenders knew the terrain, held elevated ground, and had prepared positions, while the attackers had to expose themselves approaching the fortified city. The shock of the defeat is the narrative's theological signal: YHWH has not guaranteed victory; the nation must purify itself before YHWH will act.
22: Israel encourages themselves (wayyitḥazzēq ʿam yiśrāʾēl — 'the people strengthened themselves') and resumes battle positions. ḥāzaq (H2388, to be strong/firm) is the standard encouragement vocabulary (Joshua 1:6,7,9,18; Deuteronomy 31:6-7). The resumption of the battle formation in the same place as before suggests the army maintained discipline despite the first day's losses — the military-tribal organization held despite the defeat. The willingness to return to battle is itself notable: a single-day loss of 22,000 had demoralized many ancient armies into retreat.
23: The second consultation: Israel goes up and weeps before YHWH until evening — the pattern of seeking YHWH's guidance through lamentation before a second attempt. The question — haʾôsîp lāḡešet lammilḥāmāh, 'shall I again draw near to fight?' — is a formal oracle inquiry, not a rhetorical question. YHWH's answer: 'Go up against them' (ʿălû ʾēlāyw) — the same command as the first day, with no new tactical guidance. The consultation at Bethel is described as 'before YHWH' (lipnê YHWH), confirming the ark's presence there (explicitly stated in v27).
24: The second day's battle — Israel draws near against Benjamin. The three-battle structure of the campaign parallels other biblical battle sequences where YHWH delays full deliverance to teach dependence (cf. Gideon's three-stage confirmation process, Judges 6-7). The delay is not arbitrary: YHWH's purpose in allowing Israel to suffer defeat twice before granting victory is to prevent complacency about the outcome and to ensure that Israel approaches the battle with proper humility and dependence on YHWH's power rather than numerical superiority.
25: 18,000 more Israelites fell on the second day — again 'all these drew the sword' (kullāh šōlĕpê ḥāreḇ), suggesting the casualties were experienced frontline warriors. The cumulative loss is now 40,000 men in two days: approximately 10% of the total force. At this rate of attrition, even the numerically superior Israelite force would face serious strategic problems. The two consecutive defeats are a profound humiliation for a force of 400,000 that failed to subdue one city in two days. The theological message is unambiguous: numbers do not determine battles; YHWH does.
26: The third consultation at Bethel: all Israel, all the people, went up to Bethel, wept, sat before YHWH, and fasted until evening, and offered burnt offerings and peace offerings. The triple response — weeping, fasting, sacrifice — represents the full penitential repertoire of Israel's worship (cf. Joel 2:12-15). Burnt offerings (ʿōlôt H5930 — completely consumed on the altar, expressing total devotion) and peace offerings (šəlāmîm H8002 — shared between offerer and YHWH, expressing covenant fellowship) together make the complete sacrifice. The assembly's response to two defeats is not to retreat but to deepen their engagement with YHWH.
27: The ark of the covenant's location at Bethel is explicitly confirmed: the ark of the covenant of God was there in those days, and Phinehas son of Eleazar son of Aaron ministered before it. Phinehas (pinḥās H6372) was Aaron's grandson (Exodus 6:25) and son of Eleazar who served as high priest after Aaron's death (Numbers 20:28). The Phinehas of Judges 20:28 must be either the original Phinehas (whose zeal earned him YHWH's covenant of peace, Numbers 25:10-13) or an early successor. Phinehas's intercession here echoes his previous atonement at Baal-Peor (Numbers 25:7-13; Psalms 106:30-31).
28: Phinehas's oracle inquiry: 'Shall I yet again go out to battle against Benjamin my brother, or shall I cease?' — the oracle question is phrased as a binary choice, as was standard in ANE divination. YHWH's unambiguous answer: ʿălû kî māḥār ʾettennû bəyāḏekā — 'Go up, for tomorrow I will give them into your hand.' The promise of deliverance is now explicit and time-specific: 'tomorrow.' The contrast with the previous two oracles (which gave no victory promise) signals that the condition for YHWH's active intervention has been met: Israel has sought YHWH with proper humility and sacrifice.
29: Israel sets an ambush around Gibeah (mĕʾārəḇîm) — the ambush tactic that YHWH instructed (v29, implied by v36 'the men of Israel gave ground to Benjamin because they trusted the ambush'). The ambush at Gibeah deliberately echoes Joshua's capture of Ai (Joshua 8:2-22) where Israel used the identical strategy: feigned retreat drawing out the city's defenders, then ambush forces taking the undefended city. The deliberate allusion frames Israel's action as following the same military-theological pattern as Joshua's conquest.
30: The third day's deployment: as at other times — Israel sets battle formations exactly as on days one and two, presenting the same tactical scenario to draw out Benjamin's confidence. The repetition of the same deployment was intentional deception: Benjamin had defeated Israel twice using the same approach, so repeating it made the third day look like another frontal attack. The ambush forces are already in position (v36-37) — the third battle plan is a two-part operation requiring coordinated timing between the main force and the hidden ambush units.
31: Benjamin came out against the people and was drawn away from the city — the feigned-retreat tactic successfully lures the Benjaminite forces away from their defensive positions. The roads mentioned — toward Bethel, toward Gibeah, into the open country — describe the routes Benjamin used in their pursuit of the retreating Israelites. The initial Israelite casualties (approximately 30, kəmippaʿam bəpaʿam, 'as on previous occasions') in the third day's opening are intentional: they needed to appear convincingly as another Israelite defeat to maintain the deception long enough to spring the ambush.
32: Benjamin says: 'They are routed before us as before' — their confidence in repeating the previous victories is the deception's success. Israel's ordered retreat while sustaining controlled casualties is itself a military achievement: maintaining cohesion while withdrawing under pursuit is extremely difficult. The mention of 'drawing them away from the city' confirms that the ambush tactic's first objective — separating the defenders from their fortifications — has succeeded. An ancient fortification without its garrison was nearly helpless against a determined attacker.
33: The battle reaches Baal-tamar (baʿal tāmār H1193, 'lord of the palm tree' — an unidentified site near Gibeah) — the main Israelite force turns and makes a stand. Simultaneously, the men of Israel who were in ambush rushed out of their place from Maareh-geba (mîmaʿărēh-geḇaʿ). The timing of the ambush's emergence is the critical moment: too early and Benjamin would flee back to Gibeah; too late and the main force could be broken. Ancient military coordination without radio communication depended on pre-arranged signals (smoke, flags, trumpets) or time-based triggers.
34: 10,000 chosen men of Israel came against Gibeah — the ambush force is specified as elite warriors (bāḥûr H977, chosen/selected — the same term used for the elite Benjaminite slingers in v16). The battle was severe (wəhammilḥāmāh kāḇəḏāh — 'the battle was heavy/severe'). They did not know that disaster was close (lōʾ yāḏəʿû kî-naggaʿat ʿălêhem hārāʿāh) — the Benjaminites outside the city pursuing the retreating main force do not yet realize their city is under attack from behind. The phrase 'did not know' is an ironic reversal of the Levite's concubine narrative: the perpetrators now face an unseen evil approaching them.
35: YHWH's decisive intervention: YHWH defeated Benjamin before Israel — the theological summary precedes the tactical narrative that follows. The casualty figure: 25,100 drawn-sword men of Benjamin fell on this day. Benjamin's total force was approximately 26,700 (v15-16); losing 25,100 in a single day means over 93% of the Benjaminite army was destroyed. The juxtaposition with v21 (22,000 Israelites) and v25 (18,000 Israelites) is intentional: YHWH allowed Israel to suffer 40,000 casualties over two days, then destroyed 25,100 Benjaminites in one.
36: The Israelites 'gave ground to Benjamin' (wayyittənû bənê yiśrāʾēl mākôm libinyāmîn) — the narrator reveals that the retreat was deliberate. bāṭaḥ (H982, trusted/relied on) — they relied on the ambush (hāʾōrēb). The tactical picture is now clarified retrospectively: the two-day withdrawal that appeared to be defeat was intentional maneuvering. The three-part structure — strategic retreat, surprise flank attack, burning of Gibeah — is militarily coherent and parallels Joshua's Ai campaign precisely.
37: The ambush forces rushed against Gibeah (wayyāšêṭ hāʾōrēb wayyabôʾ ʾel-giḇʿāh) — the sudden advance on the undefended city. šāṭaṭ (H7786, to rush/stretch out suddenly — a rare verb suggesting rapid movement) followed immediately by put all the city to the sword (wayyak ʾet-kol-hāʿîr lepî-ḥāreḇ). The speed of the city's capture confirms that Gibeah's garrison had been drawn out entirely by the three-day battle sequence: no resistance is described at the walls. The city walls that had protected the perpetrators are now empty.
38: The pre-arranged signal: a great cloud of smoke rising from the city (maśʾēt heʿāšān H4864 H6227). Smoke signals were standard military communication in antiquity: Joshua 8:20 uses the same signal (smoke from burning Ai) to coordinate the ambush's counterattack. Pre-arranged smoke signals required clear sightlines and pre-agreed meanings, suggesting a level of battlefield coordination between units separated by several kilometers. The smoke signal triggers the main force's turn-and-fight response simultaneously with the Benjaminites' realization of what has happened.
39: The main Israelite force turns in the battle — the feigned retreat reverses direction when the signal appears. Benjamin's confidence at this moment is at its maximum: they believe they are again routing Israel (v32). The turn is a shock: troops in pursuit suddenly face a formed enemy line turning to engage, while simultaneously seeing their city in flames. The psychological collapse of the pursuing force when they realize their city is burning behind them is a standard result of this ambush-and-fire tactic: it eliminates both the tactical and the psychological security of the defending force.
40: The smoke pillar rises from Gibeah — the city is going up in its entirety to the sky (ʿāśān haʿîr ʿālāh haššāmayim). The total destruction of Gibeah by fire is the application of the Deuteronomic ḥērem (ban) on a city whose inhabitants have committed the crime of Genesis 19 (Sodom-equivalence). The comparison of Gibeah's fate to Sodom is explicit in the narrative's allusions (Judges 19:22 — 'sons of Belial' besieging the house; Lot-like host offering daughters). The fire that destroyed Sodom now consumes Gibeah.
41: The Benjaminites' terror: Benjamin saw that disaster had struck them (wayyərʾû bənê-binyāmîn kî nəgûpāh hîʾ lāhem). nāgap (H5062, to strike/smite — used of plagues and military defeats sent by YHWH) — they recognize YHWH's judgment. The terror (ḥārēḏ H2729) is the same word as 'Harod' (the Spring of Trembling where Gideon's army drank). The Israelite main force, now turning to fight rather than flee, combined with the rising smoke from Gibeah, produces an instantaneous psychological collapse in the Benjaminite pursuit force.
42: Benjamin flees toward the wilderness road (derek hammiḏbār) — the open desert to the east, toward the Jordan Valley. Wilderness flight was the standard escape route from battle in the central hill country: the terrain was unfamiliar to pursuers but offered concealment to fugitives. The battle catches up with them: the men from the cities were destroying them in the midst of them — the ambush forces emerging from Gibeah cut off the retreat from behind while the main Israelite force pressed from in front. The classical 'hammer and anvil' tactical encirclement.
43: The Israelites encircled Benjamin, pursued and easily trodden them down from Nohah to opposite Gibeah on the east. Nohah (nôḥāh) is a site not otherwise identified. The encirclement phrase (kitrû ʾet-binyāmîn hirḏîpû mənûḥāh) uses the vocabulary of surrounding (kātar H3803, to encircle/surround) combined with pursuit (rāḏap H7291). The tactical description is a complete annihilation: encirclement, pursuit, and no avenue of escape. The specificity of 'opposite Gibeah on the east' identifies the killing ground geographically — where the retreating Benjaminites were caught between the burning city and the pursuing Israelite force.
44: 18,000 men of Benjamin fell in this phase of the battle — the initial phase of the rout. This is less than the 25,100 total (v35) and represents the first stage of casualties as the Benjaminite force disintegrated. The number 18,000 also matches the Israelite casualty figure on the second day (v25) — whether this parallel is intentional is debated. The battle's third day produces Benjaminite casualties nearly matching Israel's total losses over the first two days, demonstrating the reversal of fortunes.
45: The surviving Benjaminites flee to the wilderness toward the rock of Rimmon (ṣûr-rimmôn H7417) — identified with Rammun, a prominent rocky summit approximately 9 km east of Bethel, 600 meters above the surrounding terrain. The rock of Rimmon provided a natural fortress: steep approach, limited access routes, and visibility over the surrounding desert. 5,000 more fell on the Gidom roads, and 2,000 were cut down near Gidom — the pursuit casualties continue during the flight. The successive casualty figures (18,000 in v44; 5,000 + 2,000 = 7,100 in v45) total 25,100, matching v35's summary figure.
46: Summary of Benjamin's casualties: 25,000 drawn-sword men fell that day, all warriors. The summary restates v35's figure (25,100, with slight rounding). The complete destruction of Benjamin's military forces means that the 600 survivors at Rimmon (v47) represent the entire surviving male population of fighting age in the tribe. Benjamin has been reduced from one of Israel's tribes to a remnant of 600 men — a demographic catastrophe that Judges 21 will struggle to address.
47: 600 men turned and fled to the wilderness toward the rock of Rimmon and remained there four months. The four-month stay at Rimmon suggests they were waiting for Israel's anger to subside enough to negotiate safe passage. The rock of Rimmon as a refuge provided natural defensibility against any Israelite follow-up campaign: 600 determined defenders on a rocky summit could hold against a much larger force. The four months also gave time for the national grief and oath-crisis to develop (Judges 21) that would eventually motivate efforts to restore rather than exterminate Benjamin.
48: Israel's total destruction of Benjamin: all the cities they found, they put to the sword and burned every city (ʾēt-kol-hāʿîr ʾăšer māṣāʾû šillĕḥû bəʾēš). The campaign against Benjamin's cities is the application of the ḥērem formula (Deuteronomy 13:15-16: for an Israelite city that has apostatized, 'you shall put its inhabitants to the sword... burn the city with all its spoil'). The complete burning of all Benjaminite cities except Gibeah (already burned) represents the maximum possible covenant judgment applied to a tribe. Benjamin's entire settled infrastructure is destroyed; only the 600 at Rimmon survive.
